articles.summary: JSON-LD is a format for adding structured data to webpages that can aid web crawlers in understanding the semantic structure of your site and potentially improve search ranking. To add JSON-LD to a page, include the following script in the <head> section: `<script type="application/ld+json"> { "@context" : "https://schema.org" , "@graph" : [ { "@type" : "WebSite" , "@id" : "https://hawksley.dev/#website" , "url" : "https://hawksley.dev/" , "name" : "Ethan Hawksley" } ] }`. This allows specialized crawlers like Googlebot to parse the contents and describe your webpage.

articles.title: Prefer duplication over the wrong abstraction (2016)
articles.summary: Prefer duplication over the wrong abstraction is far cheaper than the wrong abstraction. This concept was discussed in a 2014 "all the little things" talk at RailsConf and has been revisited on this blog. Duplication involves extracting existing functionality, giving it a name, and replacing it with a new abstraction. This creates a new method or class, which can be used to solve different problems. However, as more requirements are added, the code becomes increasingly complex, making it difficult to maintain and modify. The pressure to retain existing code due to its complexity and effort expended can lead developers down a path of incompatibility and maintenance issues.

articles.title: Developers don't understand CORS (2019)
articles.summary: Developers who work with web applications often struggle to understand how Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) works, particularly when it comes to servers running on localhost. A common issue is that browsers ignore CORS policies for localhost servers, allowing them to bypass security restrictions and potentially lead to vulnerabilities. This can be attributed to the fact that localhost servers are not subject to CORS restrictions, as Chrome does respect CORS headers for these servers. To address this, a secure implementation would involve implementing a REST API with an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header set to `https://zoom.us` and a Content Security Policy header blocking rendering within iframes, thereby preventing unauthorized access to Zoom meetings.
